subsystem: TensorFlow shape-inference: InferenceContext::InternalMakeShapeFromTensor, the routine that converts a runtime shape-tensor (a rank-0 or rank-1 int32/int64 Tensor whose values ARE a shape) into a ShapeHandle during graph shape inference. It is the internal worker behind MakeShapeFromTensor and is exercised whenever an op's output shape is derived from a shape tensor (e.g. Reshape, Fill, RandomUniform, BroadcastTo).
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified method name InferenceContext::InternalMakeShapeFromTensor, the TensorFlow-specific types ShapeHandle / DimensionHandle / Tensor / DataType::DT_INT32 / DT_INT64, the helper vocabulary WithRank, Rank, Dim, ValueKnown, Value, MakeDim, UnknownDim, ReturnUnknownShape, ReturnCreatedShape, TF_RETURN_IF_ERROR, and errors::InvalidArgument. These are the shape_inference.cc APIs from tensorflow/core/framework. The in-corpus banner names the file corpus/train/train_0250_cwe400.cpp and the header comment tags it primevul_train_2047_vuln, i.e. a PrimeVul-sourced sample flagged for CWE-400 (uncontrolled resource consumption).
trust_boundary: The Tensor* t and the ShapeHandle tensor_shape both originate from a graph/model that TensorFlow treats as untrusted input: a saved model, a serialized GraphDef, or an RPC-delivered graph can carry arbitrary shape tensors and arbitrary declared shapes for those tensors. The hostile, attacker-chosen values are: (1) the VALUE stored in dimension 0 of tensor_shape (consumed as num_dims at line 20) when the concrete shape tensor is unknown at inference time, and (2) the element values and element count of the concrete tensor t on the rank-1 path (lines 68-96). Nothing between the trust boundary and this function bounds those numbers against a memory budget, so they must be treated as fully controlled 32/64-bit integers.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point returns a Status and takes four parameters. treat_unknown_scalar_tensor_as_unknown_shape is a caller policy flag that relaxes the rank requirement for the symbolic path. t is the concrete shape tensor if its values are already materialized (may be nullptr when only the tensor's shape, not its contents, is known during inference). tensor_shape is the ShapeHandle describing the SHAPE of that shape tensor (i.e. how many dimensions the output shape will have). out is an out-parameter that receives the derived ShapeHandle. The function's job is to translate the tensor's contents (or, failing that, its shape) into a symbolic output shape.

Unless the caller opted into the relaxed policy, WithRank asserts that tensor_shape has rank exactly 1 (a shape tensor is a 1-D vector of dimension sizes), rewriting tensor_shape in place with the refined handle. TF_RETURN_IF_ERROR short-circuits and returns the error Status if the rank does not match. When the flag IS set, this guard is skipped, so tensor_shape may be rank 0 on the code that follows, which is exactly what the next block reasons about.

This is the symbolic branch: the concrete contents of the shape tensor are not available (t is null), so the routine reasons only from tensor_shape. If tensor_shape itself is rank 0 (a scalar shape tensor whose one relaxed-policy value would be -1), nothing is known and it returns a fully-unknown output shape via ReturnUnknownShape. The comment 'guarded by the check above' refers to the WithRank/flag interplay at lines 6-8.

Still on the t==nullptr path, when tensor_shape is rank 1: Dim(tensor_shape, 0) extracts the (symbolic) length of the shape vector, i.e. how many output dimensions there will be. ValueKnown(shape_dim) checks whether that length is a statically-known integer; if not, an unknown shape is returned. If it IS known, num_dims = Value(shape_dim) reads the concrete integer (an int64). The function then builds a vector of num_dims placeholder dimensions: dims.reserve(num_dims) pre-allocates capacity and the loop pushes num_dims copies of UnknownDim() (a fully-unknown symbolic dimension). ReturnCreatedShape wraps that vector into a ShapeHandle. Critically, num_dims is a data-derived integer, and both the reserve and the loop scale linearly with it.

Now the concrete-contents path (t is non-null). If the tensor is rank 0 (a scalar), the only legal encoding is the sentinel value -1, meaning 'unknown shape'. For DT_INT32 and DT_INT64 the scalar<>() accessor reads the single value; if it is anything other than -1 the function fails with InvalidArgument (setting *out = nullptr first) and echoes the offending value; if it is -1 it returns an unknown shape. Any dtype other than int32/int64 is rejected. This block validates the scalar sentinel and never allocates based on the value, so it is not a resource-consumption surface.

Having handled rank 0 above, any concrete tensor that is not rank 1 is rejected here with InvalidArgument (a shape tensor must be a 1-D vector). *out is nulled and a descriptive message including the actual rank and DebugString is returned. This is pure validation; note the duplicated 'rank 0 rank 0' text is a cosmetic message typo, not a security issue.

The rank-1 concrete path. For int32 and int64 it takes a flat view of the tensor and iterates every element. Each value must be >= -1 (values below -1 are rejected as invalid shape entries); -1 is turned into an unknown dim and any non-negative value becomes a concrete dimension of that size via MakeDim(val). The resulting dims vector therefore has exactly flat_t.size() entries, which equals the actual element count of the concrete tensor. Unlike the symbolic branch, the number of iterations is bounded by the tensor's real element count (and thus by the memory the tensor already occupies), so the allocation of dims is proportional to an already-materialized buffer. Non-int32/int64 dtypes are rejected.
On success, the assembled dims vector is wrapped into a ShapeHandle and returned via out with an OK Status. This is the single success exit for both concrete rank-1 dtype branches.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core CWE-400 defect. num_dims is the statically-known VALUE of dimension 0 of tensor_shape, i.e. an attacker-chosen integer taken from the declared shape of the shape tensor in an untrusted graph. There is NO upper bound check between reading it and using it. dims.reserve(num_dims) immediately requests capacity for num_dims DimensionHandle objects, and the loop then pushes num_dims of them. An attacker who declares the shape tensor to be, say, [1000000000] causes the process to attempt to allocate on the order of num_dims * sizeof(DimensionHandle) bytes, exhausting memory and killing the process (or triggering std::bad_alloc / std::length_error). Because shape inference runs during graph loading/optimization, before any op executes, this is a cheap remote denial-of-service: a tiny malicious GraphDef forces an enormous allocation. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: reject or clamp num_dims against a sane maximum-dimensions bound (and treat it as untrusted) BEFORE calling reserve or looping. Note the value is a length, so it flows into reserve()'s size_t parameter; if Value ever returned a negative int64 it would convert to a near-SIZE_MAX request and abort immediately.

what_could_go_wrong.code: for (int i = 0; i < num_dims; i++) dims.push_back(UnknownDim());
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: A distinct integer-width defect at the same site. num_dims is an int64 (auto-deduced from Value()), but the loop counter i is a plain int (32-bit). When num_dims exceeds INT_MAX (2147483647) the comparison i < num_dims can never become false because i wraps around on overflow — signed integer overflow is undefined behavior, and in practice the loop either never terminates or oscillates, pushing UnknownDim() indefinitely until memory is exhausted. So even the DoS bound implied by reserve is undercounted: a num_dims above 2^31 turns a large-allocation into an unbounded/near-infinite loop. The fix is to bound num_dims to a small maximum and use a width-matched (int64/size_t) counter.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
for (int i = 0; i < flat_t.size(); ++i) {
      const int32_t val = flat_t(i);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: A lower-severity mirror of the loop-width issue on the concrete path (identical shape at lines 82-83 for the int64 dtype). flat_t.size() returns an int64 element count, but i is int. A tensor with more than INT_MAX elements would overflow i (undefined behavior, potential non-termination). This is materially less dangerous than the symbolic path because a tensor with 2^31+ int32 elements already occupies >8 GB of real memory that the attacker must have supplied, so the count is self-limiting by the tensor's materialized size; still, the counter type does not match the size type. Confirm the impl uses an int64/size_t index here.

Informational downstream concern (identical at line 89). On the concrete rank-1 path each non-negative val becomes a concrete dimension size with no upper bound other than INT32_MAX / INT64_MAX. This function does not allocate for the dimension value, but the huge symbolic dimension it produces can propagate to later shape arithmetic (products for element counts) where multiplication of several such large dims can overflow or drive a subsequent allocation. That risk lives in downstream consumers, not here; the only guard this function makes is val >= -1, which does not cap the magnitude. Worth confirming that downstream shape/size computations bound the total number of elements.

summary: This is TensorFlow's InternalMakeShapeFromTensor. Its dominant risk is a classic CWE-400 uncontrolled-resource-consumption / OOM DoS: on the t==nullptr symbolic branch it reads num_dims directly from an attacker-controlled shape value (line 20) and does dims.reserve(num_dims) plus a matching push loop (lines 22-23) with no upper bound, so a tiny malicious graph forces an arbitrarily large allocation during shape inference. A second, co-located integer-width bug (int loop counter against an int64 num_dims) turns counts above 2^31 into an unbounded loop. The concrete rank-1 path is comparatively safe because its work is bounded by the tensor's already-materialized element count.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: Status InferenceContext::InternalMakeShapeFromTensor(bool, const Tensor* t, ShapeHandle tensor_shape, ShapeHandle* out) (lines 2-4)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Signature itself is safe; the risk is in how the untrusted t and tensor_shape are consumed, captured in the field-level anchors.
Policy flag set by trusted caller; only relaxes a rank guard, carries no memory/size contract of its own.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: const Tensor* t (line 3) / nullptr check line 9
reviewed_anchors.reason: Null case is explicitly handled at line 9 before any dereference; no NULL-deref reachable.
Its dimension-0 value is attacker-controlled and drives the unbounded allocation.
Output handle; consistently set (including *out=nullptr on error paths) before return.
Validation helper enforcing rank==1; returns error via TF_RETURN_IF_ERROR, adds a guard rather than a hazard.
Guard that safely returns unknown shape; no contract violated.
Reads dim 0 only after rank established; index is constant 0, no OOB.
Gate that returns unknown shape when the count is symbolic; correct guard, but it does NOT bound the magnitude when the value IS known — that gap is the finding at line 20.
Attacker-controlled int64 count read with no upper bound; feeds reserve/loop.
Unbounded pre-allocation proportional to attacker value — primary CWE-400 OOM.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (int i = 0; i < num_dims; i++) dims.push_back(UnknownDim()) (line 23)
reviewed_anchors.reason: int counter vs int64 num_dims — signed overflow / unbounded loop for counts > INT_MAX, plus unbounded push.
Validates the -1 sentinel for int32/int64 and rejects other dtypes; no size-driven allocation.
Correct validation of the scalar unknown-shape sentinel; no memory contract.
Rejects non-rank-1 concrete tensors; pure validation (cosmetic 'rank 0 rank 0' message typo only).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (int i = 0; i < flat_t.size(); ++i) concrete loops (lines 71, 82)
reviewed_anchors.reason: int index vs int64 flat_t.size(); width mismatch, though bounded by tensor's real element count (lower severity).
Lower-bound validation; correct, but see MakeDim note about missing upper bound.
No upper bound on dimension magnitude; informational downstream-overflow concern.
Rejects non-int32/int64 dtypes; safe default.
Success return; safe wrapper over the assembled vector.

findings.title: Uncontrolled memory allocation from attacker-controlled shape-tensor dimension count (OOM DoS)
findings.anchor: const auto num_dims = Value(shape_dim); ... dims.reserve(num_dims); for (int i = 0; i < num_dims; i++) dims.push_back(UnknownDim());
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption (unbounded allocation from untrusted count)
findings.invariant: num_dims, derived from an untrusted graph's declared shape-tensor length, MUST be validated against a small maximum-rank bound (and rejected if negative) BEFORE it is used to reserve capacity or bound the push loop.
findings.failure_mode: On the t==nullptr branch with a known-but-huge dimension-0 value (e.g. the shape tensor declared as shape [2000000000]), reserve(num_dims) and the loop attempt to allocate/construct ~num_dims DimensionHandle entries with no cap, exhausting memory and terminating the process (bad_alloc/length_error or kernel OOM kill). Shape inference runs at graph load/optimization time, so a few-byte malicious GraphDef yields a remote denial of service before any op executes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the production shape_inference.cc adds a guard such as `if (num_dims < 0 || num_dims > kMaxRank) return errors::InvalidArgument(...)` before reserve/loop, and that num_dims is treated as untrusted.

findings.title: Signed 32-bit loop counter against int64 count -> overflow / non-terminating loop
findings.anchor: for (int i = 0; i < num_dims; i++) dims.push_back(UnknownDim());
findings.code: for (int i = 0; i < num_dims; i++) dims.push_back(UnknownDim());
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow of loop induction variable (int i vs int64 num_dims)
findings.invariant: The loop counter's type must be at least as wide as num_dims (int64/size_t) so the termination test i < num_dims is well-defined for all valid num_dims.
findings.failure_mode: num_dims is int64 but i is a 32-bit int. For num_dims > INT_MAX (2147483647), i wraps at 2^31 (signed overflow, undefined behavior); in practice the condition i < num_dims never becomes false, so the loop pushes UnknownDim() unboundedly and runs the process out of memory / hangs. This compounds the CWE-400 finding: the reserve-based DoS understates the impact because such counts also break loop termination.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the counter is widened to int64/size_t (or the num_dims cap makes >INT_MAX unreachable) in the shipped code.

findings.title: int index vs int64 element count on concrete rank-1 iteration
findings.code:
for (int i = 0; i < flat_t.size(); ++i) {
      const int32_t val = flat_t(i);
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow of loop induction variable (int index vs int64 size)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0250_cwe400.cpp (this function is the implementation; identical pattern at lines 82-83)
findings.invariant: An index iterating flat_t.size() (int64) must be int64/size_t so it cannot overflow for large tensors.
findings.failure_mode: flat_t.size() returns an int64 element count while i is a 32-bit int; a tensor with more than INT_MAX elements overflows i (undefined behavior, potential non-termination or negative index). Practically self-limited because a >2^31-element int32 tensor already occupies >8 GB of attacker-supplied memory, so severity is low, but the counter width still does not match the size type.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the shipped loops use int64/size_t indices, matching flat_t.size().

findings.title: No upper bound on concrete dimension magnitude fed to MakeDim
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on numeric magnitude (latent downstream overflow)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — downstream shape/size consumers; anchor here at lines 78 and 89
findings.invariant: Consumers that multiply these dimension sizes into an element/byte count must guard against overflow; this function only enforces val >= -1 and does not cap magnitude.
findings.failure_mode: Each non-negative val (up to INT32_MAX / INT64_MAX) becomes a concrete dimension with only a lower-bound (>= -1) check. No allocation happens here, but a produced shape with several near-max dimensions can overflow subsequent product/element-count arithmetic in downstream ops, potentially driving a bad allocation there. Informational: the risk materializes outside this function.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm downstream shape-element/byte computations bound the total number of elements and detect multiplication overflow.

audit_checklist: Verify a maximum-rank / non-negative bound check on num_dims (line 20) exists BEFORE dims.reserve and the push loop, rejecting oversized shape-tensor lengths with InvalidArgument.
Confirm the reserve+loop at lines 22-23 cannot allocate proportionally to an unbounded attacker-controlled count (the primary CWE-400 OOM surface).
Confirm the loop counter at line 23 is int64/size_t, not int, so counts > INT_MAX terminate correctly.
Confirm the concrete-path indices at lines 71 and 82 are int64/size_t to match flat_t.size().
Trace where InternalMakeShapeFromTensor's num_dims value originates (declared shape of the shape tensor) and confirm it is treated as untrusted graph input, not trusted metadata.
Verify downstream consumers of the returned ShapeHandle guard against overflow when multiplying dimension magnitudes produced via MakeDim(val).
Confirm every error path sets *out = nullptr (or leaves it defined) so callers never read an uninitialized ShapeHandle on failure.
